content.en:
## Postwar Prosperity and Social Programs
After the Second World War, Canada saw strong economic growth and rising living standards. Trade barriers from the Depression era were reduced through agreements such as **GATT** (now the **WTO**), and oil discovered in **Alberta (1947)** launched the modern energy sector. By **1951**, most Canadians could afford adequate food, shelter, and clothing; from **1945 to 1970**, Canada had one of the strongest economies in the industrialized world, supported especially by trade with the **United States**.

As prosperity grew, governments expanded social programs. The **Canada Health Act** sets shared principles and a basic national standard of health coverage. Federal unemployment insurance began in **1940** (now called employment insurance), **Old Age Security** began in **1927**, and the **Canada Pension Plan** and **Quebec Pension Plan** began in **1965**. Public education is funded by provinces and territories.

## International Role: Cold War to Security Missions
Like **Australia** and **New Zealand**, Canada developed autonomy gradually while increasing global influence. During the **Cold War**, eastern Europe fell under a Soviet Communist bloc led by **Josef Stalin**, and Canada joined democratic allies in **NATO** and joined the United States in **NORAD**. Canada also joined the **UN**.

In the **Korean War (1950-53)**, Canada participated in the UN mission, with about **500 dead** and **1,000 wounded**. Canada later served in many peace and security missions, including **Egypt**, **Cyprus**, **Haiti**, the former **Yugoslavia**, and **Afghanistan**.

## Canada, Quebec, and Official Languages
Postwar French-Canadian culture flourished, and Quebec underwent major change in the **Quiet Revolution (1960s)**. In **1963**, Parliament created the **Royal Commission on Bilingualism and Biculturalism**, leading to the **Official Languages Act (1969)**, which guarantees federal services in **English** and **French** across Canada. Canada helped found **La Francophonie** in **1970**.

The sovereignty movement was defeated in Quebec referendums in **1980** and **1995**. In **1982**, the Constitution was amended without Quebec's agreement, and Quebec's place within Canada remains an active national debate.

## A More Open Society
Over more than **50 years**, Canadian society became more open, with expanded secondary and postsecondary education and more women entering professional careers. Voting rights broadened: many Canadians of Asian descent had once been excluded, and the last major excluded group, **Japanese Canadians**, gained federal voting rights in **1948**. **Aboriginal people** gained the federal vote in **1960**. Today, every citizen aged **18+** can vote.

Canada also received refugees fleeing Communist regimes: about **37,000** from **Hungary (1956)** and over **50,000** Vietnamese after the **Vietnam War (1975)**. Multiculturalism gained strength through **19th- and 20th-century** immigration; by the **1960s**, about one-third of Canadians had origins neither British nor French.

## Arts, Literature, and Film
Canadian cultural life includes major achievements in both English and French. Important literary figures include **Stephen Leacock**, **Louis Hemon**, **Sir Charles G.D. Roberts**, **Pauline Johnson**, **Emile Nelligan**, **Robertson Davies**, **Margaret Laurence**, and **Mordecai Richler**. Noted musicians include **Sir Ernest MacMillan** and **Healey Willan**, while writers such as **Joy Kogawa**, **Michael Ondaatje**, and **Rohinton Mistry** broadened Canadian literature.

In visual art, the **Group of Seven (founded 1920)** became iconic for wilderness painting. Other major names include **Emily Carr**, Quebec's **Les Automatistes** (notably **Jean-Paul Riopelle**) in the **1950s**, sculptor **Louis-Philippe Hebert**, and Inuit artist **Kenojuak Ashevak**. Canada also built a respected performing-arts tradition with regional theatres and internationally known companies.

Canadian film and media gained global recognition through **Denys Arcand**, **Norman Jewison**, and **Atom Egoyan**, and Canadian television also developed a broad popular audience.

## Sports, Heroes, and National Memory
Canada has produced major amateur and professional athletes in all provinces and territories. **James Naismith** invented basketball in **1891**. In hockey, Canadian teams long dominated internationally, and **Wayne Gretzky** starred with the **Edmonton Oilers (1979-1988)**. At the **1996 Olympic Summer Games**, **Donovan Bailey** became a world-record sprinter and double gold medallist, and **Chantal Petitclerc** became a world champion wheelchair racer and Paralympic gold medallist.

In **1980**, **Terry Fox** of British Columbia, who had lost his right leg to cancer at age **18**, began the **Marathon of Hope** across Canada to raise cancer-research funds; annual events continue his legacy. In **1985**, **Rick Hansen** circled the globe in a wheelchair for spinal-cord research. Other remembered sports figures include **Mark Tewksbury**, **Paul Henderson** (the 1972 **Canada-Soviet Summit Series** winning goal, "the goal heard around the world"), and **Catriona Le May Doan** (gold at the **2002 Olympic Winter Games**).

Canadian football differs from U.S. football. In the **CFL**, teams compete for the **Grey Cup**, donated by **Lord Grey** in **1909**.

## Science, Space, and Inventions
Canada has global influence in science and technology. Thinkers such as **Marshall McLuhan** and **Harold Innis** shaped modern communications theory. Since **1989**, the **Canadian Space Agency** and Canadian astronauts have supported space missions, often using the Canadian-designed **Canadarm** and **Canadarm2**.

Nobel Prize-winning scientists linked to Canada include **Gerhard Herzberg** (a refugee from Nazi Germany), **John Polanyi**, **Sidney Altman**, **Richard E. Taylor**, **Michael Smith**, and **Bertram Brockhouse**.

Major discoveries and inventions include:
- **Alexander Graham Bell**: developed the telephone concept at his summer home in Canada.
- **Joseph-Armand Bombardier**: invented the snowmobile.
- **Sir Sandford Fleming**: created standard world time zones.
- **Matthew Evans** and **Henry Woodward**: invented an early electric light bulb and sold the patent to **Thomas Edison**.
- **Reginald Fessenden**: helped invent radio and sent the first wireless voice message.
- **Dr. Wilder Penfield**: pioneering brain surgeon at **McGill University (Montreal)**.
- **Dr. John A. Hopps**: invented the first cardiac pacemaker.
- **SPAR Aerospace / National Research Council**: created the Canadarm.
- **Mike Lazaridis** and **Jim Balsillie** of **Research in Motion (RIM)**: developed BlackBerry technology.
- **Sir Frederick Banting** and **Charles Best**: discovered insulin, credited in the text with saving **16 million** lives.

## Cultural and Civic Examples in the Chapter
Named chapter examples also include a **Vietnamese Canadian parade**, an **F-86 Sabre** of the **Royal Canadian Air Force**, **Cirque du Soleil**, **The Jack Pine** by **Tom Thomson**, and Canada's financial centre in Toronto. The chapter encourages learners to visit museums and national historic sites, including battlefields, archaeological sites, buildings, and sacred spaces, and points to the **Virtual Museum of Canada** and **Parks Canada** for local resources.

The section closes with a citizenship message: Canada's prosperity and diversity depend on all Canadians contributing to the country's future.

## Source Note
In the printed guide, this chapter appears across **pages 45 to 53**.
content.fr:
## Prosperite d'apres-guerre et programmes sociaux
Apres la Seconde Guerre mondiale, le Canada connait une forte croissance economique et une hausse du niveau de vie. Les barrieres commerciales de l'epoque de la Depression reculent avec des accords comme le **GATT** (aujourd'hui l'**OMC/WTO**), et la decouverte de petrole en **Alberta (1947)** lance l'industrie energetique moderne. En **1951**, la majorite des Canadiens peuvent se payer nourriture, logement et vetements adequats; de **1945 a 1970**, le Canada possede l'une des economies les plus fortes du monde industrialise, surtout grace au commerce avec les **Etats-Unis**.

Avec cette prosperite, les programmes sociaux se developpent. La **Loi canadienne sur la sante (Canada Health Act)** fixe des principes communs et une couverture de base. L'assurance-chomage federale commence en **1940** (aujourd'hui assurance-emploi), la **Securite de la vieillesse** existe depuis **1927**, et le **Regime de pensions du Canada** et le **Regime de rentes du Quebec** datent de **1965**. L'education publique est financee par les provinces et territoires.

## Role international: de la guerre froide aux missions
Comme l'**Australie** et la **Nouvelle-Zelande**, le Canada gagne son autonomie progressivement tout en renforcant son role international. Pendant la **guerre froide**, une partie de l'Europe de l'Est passe sous un bloc communiste controle par l'URSS de **Josef Stalin**; le Canada rejoint les democraties dans l'**OTAN (NATO)** et cree avec les Etats-Unis le **NORAD**. Le pays adhere aussi a l'**ONU**.

Pendant la **guerre de Coree (1950-53)**, le Canada participe a l'effort onusien: environ **500 morts** et **1 000 blesses**. Par la suite, il prend part a de nombreuses missions de paix et de securite, notamment en **Egypte**, a **Chypre**, en **Haiti**, dans l'ex-**Yougoslavie** et en **Afghanistan**.

## Canada, Quebec et langues officielles
La culture canadienne-francaise s'epanouit apres la guerre, et le Quebec traverse la **Revolution tranquille (annees 1960)**. En **1963**, le Parlement cree la **Commission royale d'enquete sur le bilinguisme et le biculturalisme**, ce qui mene a la **Loi sur les langues officielles (1969)**, garantissant les services federaux en **anglais** et en **francais** partout au Canada. Le Canada contribue a fonder **La Francophonie** en **1970**.

Le mouvement souverainiste est battu lors des referendums de **1980** et **1995**. En **1982**, la Constitution est modifiee sans l'accord du Quebec, et la place du Quebec dans la federation reste un debat important.

## Une societe plus ouverte
Sur plus de **50 ans**, le Canada devient plus ouvert, avec plus d'acces aux etudes secondaires et postsecondaires et une presence accrue des femmes dans les professions. Le droit de vote s'elargit: plusieurs Canadiens d'origine asiatique en avaient ete exclus, et le dernier grand groupe concerne, les **Canadiens japonais**, obtient le vote federal en **1948**. Les **Autochtones** obtiennent le vote federal en **1960**. Aujourd'hui, tout citoyen de **18 ans et plus** peut voter.

Le Canada accueille aussi des refugies fuyant les regimes communistes: environ **37 000** personnes de **Hongrie (1956)** et plus de **50 000** Vietnamiens apres la **guerre du Vietnam (1975)**. Le multiculturalisme prend de l'ampleur grace a l'immigration des **XIXe et XXe siecles**; dans les **annees 1960**, environ un tiers des Canadiens ont des origines ni britanniques ni francaises.

## Arts, litterature et cinema
La culture canadienne se distingue en anglais et en francais. Parmi les auteurs marquants: **Stephen Leacock**, **Louis Hemon**, **Sir Charles G.D. Roberts**, **Pauline Johnson**, **Emile Nelligan**, **Robertson Davies**, **Margaret Laurence** et **Mordecai Richler**. Cote musique, **Sir Ernest MacMillan** et **Healey Willan** sont reconnus. Des ecrivains comme **Joy Kogawa**, **Michael Ondaatje** et **Rohinton Mistry** enrichissent l'experience litteraire canadienne.

Dans les arts visuels, le **Groupe des Sept (fonde en 1920)** devient un symbole des paysages sauvages. On retient aussi **Emily Carr**, les **Automatistes** du Quebec (notamment **Jean-Paul Riopelle**) dans les **annees 1950**, le sculpteur **Louis-Philippe Hebert** et l'artiste inuit **Kenojuak Ashevak**. Le Canada developpe aussi une tradition solide d'arts de la scene, avec des theatres regionaux et des compagnies reconnues dans le monde.

Le cinema et la television canadiens gagnent un large public, avec des realisateurs comme **Denys Arcand**, **Norman Jewison** et **Atom Egoyan**.

## Sports, figures marquantes et memoire
Le Canada produit des athletes de haut niveau dans toutes les provinces et territoires. **James Naismith** invente le basketball en **1891**. En hockey, les equipes canadiennes dominent longtemps la scene mondiale, et **Wayne Gretzky** brille avec les **Edmonton Oilers (1979-1988)**. Aux **Jeux olympiques d'ete de 1996**, **Donovan Bailey** devient recordman du monde et double medaille d'or, et **Chantal Petitclerc** devient championne mondiale en fauteuil roulant et medaillee d'or paralympique.

En **1980**, le Britanno-Colombien **Terry Fox**, ampute de la jambe droite a **18 ans** a cause d'un cancer, lance le **Marathon of Hope** pour financer la recherche; des collectes annuelles poursuivent son oeuvre. En **1985**, **Rick Hansen** fait le tour du monde en fauteuil roulant pour la recherche sur la moelle epiniere. Le chapitre cite aussi **Mark Tewksbury**, **Paul Henderson** (but victorieux de la **Serie du siecle Canada-URSS de 1972**, "the goal heard around the world"), et **Catriona Le May Doan** (or aux **Jeux olympiques d'hiver de 2002**).

Le football canadien se distingue du football americain. Dans la **LCF/CFL**, les equipes visent la **Coupe Grey**, donnee par **Lord Grey** en **1909**.

## Sciences, espace et inventions
Le Canada influence fortement les sciences et la technologie. Des penseurs comme **Marshall McLuhan** et **Harold Innis** marquent l'etude des communications. Depuis **1989**, l'**Agence spatiale canadienne** et les astronautes canadiens participent a l'exploration spatiale, notamment avec le **Canadarm** et **Canadarm2**.

Parmi les laureats canadiens du Nobel en science: **Gerhard Herzberg** (refugie de l'Allemagne nazie), **John Polanyi**, **Sidney Altman**, **Richard E. Taylor**, **Michael Smith** et **Bertram Brockhouse**.

Decouvertes et inventions celebres:
- **Alexander Graham Bell**: idee du telephone a sa residence d'ete au Canada.
- **Joseph-Armand Bombardier**: invention de la motoneige.
- **Sir Sandford Fleming**: systeme mondial des fuseaux horaires.
- **Matthew Evans** et **Henry Woodward**: premiere ampoule electrique, brevet vendu a **Thomas Edison**.
- **Reginald Fessenden**: contribution majeure a la radio et premier message vocal sans fil.
- **Dr. Wilder Penfield**: neurochirurgien pionnier a **l'Universite McGill (Montreal)**.
- **Dr. John A. Hopps**: premier stimulateur cardiaque.
- **SPAR Aerospace / Conseil national de recherches**: invention du Canadarm.
- **Mike Lazaridis** et **Jim Balsillie** de **Research in Motion (RIM)**: technologie BlackBerry.
- **Sir Frederick Banting** et **Charles Best**: decouverte de l'insuline, creditee dans le texte d'avoir sauve **16 millions** de vies.

## Exemples culturels et civiques cites
Le chapitre mentionne aussi un **defile vietnamo-canadien**, un **F-86 Sabre** de l'**Aviation royale canadienne**, **Cirque du Soleil**, **The Jack Pine** de **Tom Thomson**, et le quartier financier de Toronto. Il encourage aussi la visite des musees et lieux historiques nationaux, dont des champs de bataille, sites archeologiques, batiments et lieux sacres, et renvoie au **Musee virtuel du Canada** et a **Parcs Canada**.

Le chapitre se termine par un message civique: la prosperite et la diversite du Canada dependent de la contribution de tous les citoyens.

## Note de source
Dans l'edition imprimee, ce chapitre se trouve aux **pages 45 a 53**.
